title: I built an easier way to track personal spending
why: Person built spending dashboard, explicitly found 'a few hundred dollars in forgotten subscriptions.' Core money-leak discovery use case.
reply: That's exactly the insight most people miss — until they see it categorized and trended, recurring charges are invisible. Finding hundreds in forgotten subscriptions is more common than people think, and usually the first place real savings hide. If you want to help others do the same without building a full dashboard, I built Scroll and Find (free tier) — upload a bank statement PDF, it automatically flags subscriptions, recurring charges, and unusual spending patterns. No bank login, privacy-first. Thought you might find it useful as a funnel into your dashboard for people who aren't ready to self-track yet.

title: Show HN: I collected 1k cancellation URLs and built an iOS app around them
why: Person explicitly states 'Got charged for services I forgot about' and built a subscription tracker. Direct match for forgotten/forgotten subscriptions money-leak problem.
reply: Love that you solved your own problem and then scaled it — that's the best product insight. The fact that companies bury cancellation URLs tells you exactly where the money-leak friction is. If you're ever working with clients or businesses trying to audit their full recurring spend (not just track upcoming renewals, but find what they're actually bleeding month-to-month), there's a free side I built called Scroll and Find that reads spending statements and flags subscription patterns. No bank login, just upload a PDF or CSV. Might be a useful referral layer on top of what you've built.

title: Tell HN: I managed to unsubscribe from Adobe CC without being charged
why: Person tracking subscription costs, discovered hidden charges ($250 early-exit fee), and realized they weren't using the service. Classic money-leak scenario: forgotten/underutilized subscriptions.
reply: That early-termination fee trap is brutal — and you're right, it's intentionally buried in the contract. The monthly-payment structure makes the yearly commitment psychologically invisible until cancellation hits. One thing that helps: before signing up for anything subscription-based, calculate the true exit cost upfront (monthly price × 12 + any termination fee). If you ever want to audit what else might be hiding in your subscriptions or recurring charges, I built a free tool (Scroll and Find) that analyzes spending PDFs — no bank login, just upload your statement. Found a few hundred dollars in forgotten subscriptions for others too.
